INLS 759 · Community Archives · SILS UNC · 2026

Video games are not preserved in a single archive. This exhibit maps three distinct environments where preservation happens, each sustaining a different part of what games mean, and how they survive across time.

scroll to begin reading
AuthorsCooley · Freeman · Wimbish
CourseINLS 759 · Community Archives
InstitutionSILS, UNC Chapel Hill
Year2026
Where Preservation Happens

Three volumes. Three environments. One distributed archive.

Video game preservation does not happen in a single place. Institutional archives collect objects and documents. Community networks sustain access and circulation. Modding communities transform games and generate new creative cultures. Each environment preserves something the others cannot. Together, they constitute a distributed infrastructure of care.

Volume I
INST
Institutional
Archives & Museums

How formal institutions collect games as cultural artifacts, preserving hardware, software, and documentation for long-term access and scholarship.

Objects & Documents Volume II
COMM
Community
Preservation
Infrastructures

How grassroots networks including ROM communities, emulation projects, and distributed archives maintain access to playable games outside formal institutions.

Access & Circulation Volume III
MOD
Modding
Communities

How players transform games and create new forms of play, building repositories, documentation, and creative cultures that preserve what institutions miss.

Creative Practice
Vol. I
Environment 01 · Institutional Archives
Institutional
Archives
& Museums

According to a 2023 study by the Video Game History Foundation, 87% of video games released in the United States before 2010 have been lost or are at risk of being lost due to lack of preservation (Salvador, 2023). Institutional video game archiving centers on the preservation and documentation of video games and their histories. Archivists must tackle digital obsolescence, aging hardware, and the fragility of physical media, while also collecting documents, first-hand accounts from developers, and promotional materials to capture the full cultural context of games. Video games, like any other forms of art or social creation, are a representation of the culture and time in which they were created. The recording of these digital artifacts is paramount.

The Strong National Museum of Play, Rochester, New York — 2025 expansion
The Scope of Collection

Video game archives collect widely: records-based institutions like the VGHF preserve design documents, concept art, promotional materials, manuals, and industry publications. One example is the Andrew Nelson Papers, a collection documenting the work of a creative director at CyberFlix, a prominent 1990s game studio — pitch documents, concept art, and production records that illuminate how games were actually made. Physical and digital games are collected across formats, from cartridges and discs to digital downloads, alongside the hardware needed to run them. Archivists use software re-compilation, emulation, and digitization to ensure games remain accessible as original platforms become obsolete. Software re-compilation refers to taking original source code and re-compiling it for newer platforms, making necessary changes to run on modern hardware. Emulation recreates the behavior of original hardware in software, allowing games to run on systems they were never designed for. Metadata and provenance, including creation date, medium, and donor history, are central to the organizational structure of these collections.

Andrew Nelson Papers — "Romanov Game," partial game pitch, circa 1997. Video Game History Foundation Library.
Acquisition, Organization, and Use

Most institutional archives follow guidelines established by the Library of Congress, including its National Digital Information Infrastructure and Preservation Program (NDIIPP) and Recommended Formats Statement for software and video games. Funding comes through donations, grants, and community support, though federal assistance has dwindled significantly in recent years. On-site engagement is often the richest experience, allowing physical interaction with consoles and memorabilia. Many collections are solely digital, with physical inventory held in remote storage. Copyright restrictions limit broad digital access, and many institutions require advance contact to arrange consultations and viewings.

Item records typically include metadata, provenance, and item details — description, medium, creation date — organized using subject headings and a classification system based on the Library of Congress. Some collections are grouped by donor name, as with the Andrew Nelson Papers. In some cases digital information about the game itself is available, though this remains the exception rather than the rule.

Strong Museum catalog record — Pac-Man Midway tabletop arcade, Coleco Industries, 1981. Object Name, Subject, Dates, Manufacturer, Material, and Origin documented.
Case Study
Preserving Virtual Worlds

In collaboration with the Rochester Institute of Technology, Stanford University, the University of Maryland, and the University of Illinois, the Library of Congress funded a two-year, $590,000 project called "Preserving Virtual Worlds" (2008–2010). The project explored preservation methods for digital games, interactive fiction, and shared virtual spaces spanning games produced between 1962 and 2003. Its main goals were developing basic standards for metadata and content representation, and conducting archiving case studies for early video games, electronic literature, and Second Life. As MITH described at the time: "This award places MITH and its partners at the forefront of those addressing increasingly urgent questions involving the preservation of creative works that are born digital." The project identified key preservation challenges, including hardware and software obsolescence, and contributed foundational frameworks still referenced in institutional game preservation today.

Screenshot of institution interface or exhibit
Community Impact

Institutional archives do not exist in isolation. Their collections are shaped by the communities that donate to them, use them, and advocate for them.

In 2025, the Strong Museum of Play expanded its collection by 570,000 items, served over 100,000 children and adults through community outreach, and hired 400 student volunteers for programming. Despite the institutional constraints that limit access, these archives remain deeply community-oriented, sustained by a network of archivists, developers, enthusiasts, and donors who see games as worth keeping.

Legal and Structural Constraints

The same legal and financial pressures that shape what institutions collect also determine who can access what they hold.

Copyright law remains the central constraint on institutional game preservation. Despite holding digitized collections, most archives can only provide on-site access, limiting reach to those who can visit in person. Federal funding has also dwindled significantly in recent years, making institutions increasingly dependent on donors and community support. As one report notes, this creates a preservation landscape that is "extremely important and challenging," with materials susceptible to deterioration, obsolescence, and significant storage demands.

→

Institutions preserve objects and documents, but access remains constrained. Where formal archives reach their limits, community networks take over, maintaining the circulation and playability that institutions cannot provide.

Vol. II
Environment 02 · Community Preservation Infrastructures
Community
Preservation
Infrastructures

Community preservation infrastructures function as distributed preservation systems that sustain access to video games when institutional archives cannot. Built outside formal institutions, these networks prioritize playability as a form of access. Through coordinated volunteer labor and distributed technical systems, they maintain continuity across unstable hardware and software environments.

These systems can also be understood as forms of community archives, where preservation is defined and enacted by participants rather than institutions. Together, they operate as a layered infrastructure, combining verification and distribution to support long-term usability rather than storage alone.

Hero screenshot
e.g. Internet Archive ROM library
or No-Intro DAT-o-MATIC interface
The Verification Layer

Projects such as No-Intro and Redump produce standardized metadata systems, DAT files that record filenames, checksums, and cryptographic hashes for each known game revision. These practices establish a form of quality control, aligning with archival principles of fixity and authenticity without centralized oversight.

No-Intro currently maintains verified sets for over 100 distinct gaming systems, from the Atari 2600 to the Nintendo Switch. Its DAT-o-MATIC database allows users to browse, search, and download metadata files for any supported system, each a structured record of every known game revision, variant, and regional release. Redump focuses specifically on optical disc media, treating each disc as a physical object with its own track layout, mastering quirks, and regional pressing history. Together these projects constitute something close to a distributed catalog of the commercial game record.

This work depends on ongoing community labor. Contributors continually verify and correct records, maintaining accuracy and completeness across thousands of titles. No-Intro and Redump contributors donate time not to play games, but to document them, updating records, flagging bad dumps, and maintaining standards no institution asked them to keep. Preservation here is not static but sustained.

Screenshot
No-Intro DAT-o-MATIC or Redump database
The Access Layer

Access platforms, including the Internet Archive software collections, provide large-scale distribution environments. Their design emphasizes usability, low barriers to entry, and compatibility with emulation tools, positioning access itself as a preservation outcome.

The Internet Archive's software library includes several dedicated game collections, among them the Console Living Room, which offers in-browser playback of thousands of console titles, and the Historical Software Collection. These are among the only spaces where a researcher can access a verified game file, run it in an emulator, and cite the source, all in one place, without institutional affiliation or on-site access.

Interface design, folder structure, and searchability shape how collections are experienced. Preservation extends beyond storing files to maintaining the systems that allow users to locate, interpret, and use them.

Screenshot
Internet Archive software library or similar
Case Study
Myrient: Failure and Redistribution

When Myrient, a 390TB ROM hosting platform, went offline in March 2026, community members mobilized within 24 hours. Within 16 days, a full mirror of 385TB was completed and redistributed through torrent-based systems.

This event demonstrates how these infrastructures operate under conditions of failure. Rather than relying on a single host, preservation shifted to a network of participants, each responsible for sustaining a portion of the archive.

Coordination occurred across community spaces, including forums and Discord servers, showing that preservation is organized socially as well as technically. Participants replicated Myrient's organizational structure and navigation, preserving the experience of access alongside the data.

Archived Myrient interface (Wayback Machine) + Minerva Archive site
Financial and Participatory Precarity

These infrastructures are both resilient and unstable. They rely on volunteer labor and operate without legal protection, depending on ongoing participation to remain accessible.

In 2024, the U.S. Copyright Office declined to expand exemptions for remote access to game collections. Myrient's shutdown reflects financial precarity rather than legal enforcement, with rising costs and declining support making centralized hosting unsustainable.

Its torrent-based afterlife distributes risk but introduces new dependencies. Long-term access is no longer a question of storage, but of sustained participation.

These systems sustain access, but they cannot guarantee it. They persist anyway.

→

Community infrastructures maintain access and circulation, making games playable. Modding communities take that playability further, transforming games and developing their own cultures of documentation and creative practice.

Vol. III
Environment 03 · Modding Communities
Modding
Communities

When a game goes unpatched, untranslated, or unplayable on modern hardware, modding communities often step in. Players who modify games are not typically thinking about preservation — they are fixing bugs, expanding stories, removing barriers, or reshaping mechanics to suit new audiences. But the cumulative effect of that labor is archival. Modding communities sustain games that institutions do not prioritize and that community infrastructures alone cannot make playable. Repositories like Mod DB and Nexus Mods serve as the primary centralized archives for this work.

Nexus Mods home page — mods and collections for 4,577 games
Mods as Preservation Practice

Mods preserve gaming culture in several distinct ways. Quality-of-life mods improve player interactions and frequently include compatibility patches that allow older games to run on newer hardware, letting 20-year-old games stand alongside modern releases. A representative example is MH2Modern, a mod for Manhunt 2 that addresses frame pacing, input handling, audio compatibility, and Windows behavior on modern systems — work the original publisher never provided, but that a single modder contributed freely. "Total conversion" mods go further still, creating entirely new gaming experiences on top of existing engines — new content, new mechanics, new worlds. Language translation mods, of which Mod DB and Nexus Mods house hundreds of thousands, extend access to games that were never officially localized; without native, fluent speakers contributing this labor, these games would remain inaccessible to entire language communities. Accessibility mods address visual and audio impairments and learning disabilities, manifesting game cues in ways more accessible to a wider range of players. Each of these forms of modding enacts core archival values: access, usability, and longevity.

Accessibility mods — visual indicators, audio cues, and dyslexia-friendly fonts
MH2Modern — a quality-of-life and compatibility patch for Manhunt 2
Repositories and Community Infrastructure

Mod DB and Nexus Mods function as the primary community-maintained repositories for game mods, hosting everything from bug fixes to total conversions. Mods are uploaded, versioned, and documented by their creators, with community comments and ratings serving as informal peer review. Beyond repositories, modding communities form around specific games, tools, and engines, with wikis, Discord servers, and forums preserving institutional knowledge about how to create, install, and maintain mods. This participatory infrastructure sustains modding culture even when original platforms disappear.

Language translation mods — Hungarian, Turkish, and Chinese translations for Stardew Valley and Starfield
Case Study
Garry's Mod

Garry's Mod, initially created in 2006, is a sandbox environment built as a modification of Half-Life 2, made up of Valve game assets and custom content uploaded by players. It persists today due to its flexible structure and consistent community contribution. By becoming a game designed around modifications, it evolved into a digital playground — a wealth of built-in assets that players use however they see fit.

Some of the most popular servers include Prop Hunt (players disguise themselves as environment objects and hide from seekers), DarkRP (role-playing games), and TTT, or Trouble in Terrorist Town, a social deduction format akin to Mafia that likely contributed to the success of multiplayer games like Among Us. Garry's Mod also serves as a creative environment for improv role-playing stories enacted by streamers, providing assets that allow for character and world building.

While Half-Life 2 is undeniably important and influential, Garry's Mod has outlived its source game by being easily transformable and customizable — making it ideal for modern content-creation culture and a defining example of how modding generates entirely new cultural forms (Nelson, 2017).

Garry's Mod — the sandbox environment built on Half-Life 2 that has outlived its source game
Accessibility mods — visual indicators, audio cues, and dyslexia-friendly fonts
Legal and Creative Precarity

The preservation value of modding exists in direct tension with the legal frameworks governing the games being modified.

Video game mods are, as Kretzschmar and Stanfill (2019) put it, "roughly all equally (il)legal." Modders do not possess rights to the intellectual property they are modifying, and through modification they often violate the Digital Millennium Copyright Act, the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, and end-user license agreements. This applies equally to creative mods, translation mods, and accessibility mods. Continued access to mods is therefore entirely dependent on publishers choosing not to exercise legal recourse — a precarity that mirrors, and in some ways exceeds, that of other community preservation infrastructures.

"I saw [modding] as a great opportunity to get some real experience and something to be able to show as a reference when applying to game designer jobs. I would some day like to work in the game industry, and creating add-on levels is a good way of exhibiting and increasing my skills and adding content to my resume." Anonymous modders, quoted in Postigo, 2007
* * * * *
The End of the Collection

Video game preservation is not a single practice. It is institutional and informal, technical and social, centralized and distributed. The three environments documented here are not alternatives to one another, they are layers. Institutions provide legitimacy and longevity. Community infrastructures provide access and circulation. Modding communities provide transformation and continuity. None of them alone is sufficient. Together, they constitute something like an archive, incomplete, precarious, and sustained by care.
